title: About Me
text:
I am an Alaska-born artist and designer based in Portland, Oregon. I currently divide my time between costume design for theatres around Portland and acting as Wardrobe Manager for Lakewood Center for the Arts in Lake Oswego. You may recognize my work from Measure for Measure and The Wolves at Portland State University; A Home What Howls at Milagro Theatre; A Design for Living at Imago Theatre; or Jersey Boys, Rossam’s Universal Robots, Groundhog Day: The Musical, and Dracula at Lakewood Center for the Arts.
As a student at Portland State University, I performed a wide variety of roles from Wardrobe Technician to Lead Costume Designer, culminating in the opportunity to design the West Coast premiere of Aditi Brennan Kapil’s translation of Measure for Measure. I earned my B.F.A. in June 2024 from the University Honors College with the thesis Exploring Gender in Classical Music through Coded Weaving.
Outside of theatre, I create art utilizing weaving, dyeing, and garment making to explore the gendered history of textiles.

title: Textile Design
text:
Previous


 Next


 Mozart — Symphony No. 1   Cotton and Wool Yarn 

[ + ] Details



This weave is a partial translation of the first violin line from Mozart’s Symphony No. 1. The pitch of a note is indicated using the color and placement of the thread. The loom was programmed with a nine-pedal pattern. This tapestry uses a cotton warp and a wool weft. When each pedal is pressed down it lifts the designated warp threads. When a weft thread is placed underneath the lifted thread, it leaves some portions of the weft exposed. This creates a pattern of exposed warp (vertical threads) and weft (horizontal threads) that is slightly different depending on which pedal has been pressed. Every note in the key signature was assigned to each pedal. This results in each note leaving a slightly different pattern on the final weave. Rests and silences in the music are indicated using a thin white thread in a plain weave. Plain weave is the most basic weave pattern in which every other warp thread is lifted. The final weave uses three colors: dark green, mid-tone green, and yellow. These colors indicate which octave a note falls into, with the dark green thread being the lowest octave and the yellow thread being the highest. For example, if one were to weave a “D” on the staff followed by a “D” below the staff, the same pedal would be pressed both times, but the first would be strung with a mid-tone green and the second with a darker green.
The duration of each note is indicated by how many times the weft string passes over the warp. In the final weaving, each pass over the warp was equivalent to half a beat. For example, two passes over the warp would indicate a quarter note. After each note was placed into the weaving, a thin white thread would be threaded in between each note to act as a spacing between each note. For notes shorter than half a beat, a colored thread would be strung across a portion of the warp, with the rest of that line being filled in by a note spacing thread. For example, a sixteenth note would be strung across exactly half of the warp before being filled in with a spacer. This spacing thread allows the viewer to determine the beginning and end of each note and differentiate notes from one another. The end of each measure is indicated by two passes of a thick white yarn. This acts as an indicator of time signature. One could count the number of threads between each measure indicator to determine how many beats fit into each measure. Once all these elements have been combined and applied to a piece of music, an abstract woven pattern begins to form.

title: a home what howls
text:
Inspired by the true story of the destruction of the Chavez Ravine in Los Angeles, a home what howls (or the house what was ravine) follows the protagonist Soledad Vargas as she fights injustice and bureaucracy in her city’s government in the years following the destruction of her family home.
The design process began by pulling reference images from archival footage of the Mission District in 1970s San Fransico, another predominantly Latino neighborhood in Southern California affected by gentrification. These images were then compared to those found in modern department store catalogues associated with the working class, such as JCPenney and Old Navy. By making this comparison, we find stylistic overlaps, creating a timeless silhouette that honors the history the work was based on while emphasizing the story’s modern relevance.
An earth-toned color palette, organic patterns, and natural fibers were selected to represent a connection to the earth and create a stark contrast to the industrial navy and gray tones worn by the White employees of the city government. Our protagonist, Soledad Vargas, exists between these two worlds, wearing cotton denim textures and floral prints in a predominantly blue palette.
The character that exists as the emotional centerpiece of the play, Coyotl, is intended to appear as an extension of the earth. Her distressed, sheer dress is embellished with green moss textures using a combination of felting and embroidery techniques as well as moss itself. The striking coyote mask was built by Prop Master Sarah Rowell and embellished by me using a variety of ethically sourced animal furs to create an amorphous animal silhouette.

title: a home what howls
text:
Design Statement
Inspired by the true story of the destruction of the Chavez Ravine in Los Angeles, a home what howls (or the house what was ravine) follows the protagonist Soledad Vargas as she fights injustice and bureaucracy in her city’s government in the years following the destruction of her family home.
The design process began by pulling reference images from archival footage of the Mission District in 1970s San Fransico, another predominantly Latino neighborhood in Southern California affected by gentrification. These images were then compared to those found in modern department store catalogues associated with the working class, such as JCPenney and Old Navy. By making this comparison, we find stylistic overlaps, creating a timeless silhouette that honors the history the work was based on while emphasizing the story’s modern relevance.
An earth-toned color palette, organic patterns, and natural fibers were selected to represent a connection to the earth and create a stark contrast to the industrial navy and gray tones worn by the White employees of the city government. Our protagonist, Soledad Vargas, exists between these two worlds, wearing cotton denim textures and floral prints in a predominantly blue palette.
The character that exists as the emotional centerpiece of the play, Coyotl, is intended to appear as an extension of the earth. Her distressed, sheer dress is embellished with green moss textures using a combination of felting and embroidery techniques as well as moss itself. The striking coyote mask was built by Prop Master Sarah Rowell and embellished by me using a variety of ethically sourced animal furs to create an amorphous animal silhouette.
